results.query: Why did Apex decide to build SAML 2.0 SSO?
results.answer:
Apex decided to build SAML 2.0 SSO because the lack of it directly caused the loss or delay of three enterprise deals in Q2 2025 — Kroger ($220K), Estée Lauder ($190K), and Carrefour ($175K) — totalling $585K in pipeline impact. Enterprise IT security teams were blocking vendor approvals without SAML in place, and Kavitha Suresh escalated the issue to CPO Priya Sharma in July 2025. The decision (PROD-1623) was owned by Ananya Singh and approved by Priya Sharma on August 22, 2025, with the rationale that SAML SSO was table-stakes for enterprise deals, not an optional feature.

Decision confirme

llmScores.notes: Answer directly addresses the query with comprehensive, well-sourced information from the NOTION document on SSO & Enterprise Security, including specific deal names, amounts, decision owners, and approval dates.

results.query: What did the build vs buy AI analysis conclude?
results.answer: **Yes, this research exists.** Rahul Verma (CTO) authored the Build vs Buy analysis on **February 28, 2026**, with a decision required by April 30, 2026 (Priya Sharma to approve) — it lives in Google Docs titled *"Build vs Buy — AI Sentiment Analysis for Apex Insights v2."* The analysis evaluated three options — building in-house, buying a specialist NLP vendor, and using an LLM API — and recommended **Option 3: Claude API**, based on a validated accuracy of **94.2%** (from spike PROD-1589), a cost of **$0.0018/review (~$18,000/month at 10M reviews)**, and a 4–6 week build timeline versus 12–1

results.query: Why was the mobile app deprioritised in H1 2026?
results.answer: The native mobile app was deprioritised by **Priya Sharma (CPO)** in a decision dated **October 8, 2025** (documented in PROD-1834 and confirmed in the H1 2026 roadmap). The core reasons were: no churn risk (0 of 14 requesting customers said they would churn without it, per a survey by **Arjun Mehta, Head of CS**), high engineering cost (6–8 months for a 3-person team for iOS + Android), and opportunity cost — those same engineers were needed for **Visual UGC video (PROD-1456)** and **Walmart real-time API (PROD-1342)**, both carrying direct ARR impact. sourceTitles: Mobile App for Brand Managers — Deprioritisation Decision

results.query: What is Apex's FY 2026 strategy and how do the Q2 OKRs connect to it?
results.answer: Apex's FY 2026 strategy, authored by Vikram Nair, targets growth from $19.2M to $27M ARR (40%) by pursuing three bets: winning the Top 50 Global Retailers, launching AI-Powered Review Intelligence (Apex Insights v2), and closing the feature gap on Visual UGC within 6 months to defend against Yotpo, Bazaarvoice, and PowerReviews. The Q2 OKRs map directly to these bets: O1 (win 8 new retailer logos, including Kroger at $240K ACV and Costco at $280K ACV) executes Bet 1; O2 (launch video reviews via PROD-1456 by April 30 for L'Oréal's pilot at $180K ACV contingent) executes Bet 3; and O4 (maintain

llmScores.notes: The answer is comprehensive and directly addresses the query with strong document support. Minor concern: 'GTM Motion 3 expansion target' phrasing is inferred rather than explicitly stated as such in the documents, though the underlying facts are accurate.

results.query: What happened during the Syndication outage and what decisions came out of it?
results.answer: On March 12, 2026, a Kafka partition count increase from 12 to 24 triggered a consumer group rebalance that lasted 4 hours 22 minutes (09:14–13:36 IST), causing real-time syndication to go fully down for Walmart, Target, and Home Depot — delaying 23,700 reviews in total with no data loss. The incident was owned by Rajan Krishnamurthy (Head of Engineering), and the root cause was that the change management process did not require an off-hours window for Kafka partition changes. Two immediate action items were completed by March 25: adding Kafka partition changes to the high-risk change list (re
sourceTitles: Pricing Model Decision — Seat-based vs Usage-based vs Module-based

results.query: billing
results.answer: The relevant document is **"Billing Migration to Stripe — Decision Log"**. Apex migrated from Chargebee to Stripe Billing, completing the migration on **August 31, 2025**, covering **240 enterprise customer subscriptions**. Key outcomes included invoice sync errors dropping from **20% to 0.3%**, billing incident response improving from **4–6 days to same-day**, and a **60% reduction in manual billing reconciliation time** for the Finance team. The migration was driven by Chargebee's inability to support the new module-based pricing model, unreliable Salesforce sync, and slow support response t

results.query: api
results.answer:
The most relevant document is **"API v2 — Launch Plan & v1 Deprecation"**. API v2 is currently in development, delayed from Q1 to Q3 2026 due to engineering resources being reallocated to Visual UGC video (PROD-1456), which is tied to a $180K L'Oréal contract. Key v2 features include GraphQL + REST support, webhooks, bulk endpoints (up to 10,000 reviews per request), OAuth 2.0 authentication, and raised rate limits (1,000 req/min standard; 10,000 req/min enterprise). v1 sunset is set for December 31, 2026, with 87 customers currently on v1.

Owner: Rahul Verma · Last updated: December 20, 2025
sourceTitles: API v2 — Launch Plan & v1 Deprecation
Apex Insights v2 — AI Sentiment Analysis PRD

results.query: permissions
results.answer: The **Permissions & Role Management Redesign PRD** covers a full overhaul of the current 3-role system (Admin, Editor, Viewer) into five new roles: Super Admin, Brand Admin, Moderator, Analyst, and Integration. A key driver was Walmart's legal team flagging a data exposure risk between competing brand teams, escalated by Arjun Mehta in November 2025; the solution enforces brand and retailer isolation at the **database query level** with a full audit log for compliance. results.query: What is the current status of the Home Depot pilot?
results.answer:
Based on the **H1 2026 Product Roadmap** and **Q1 2026 Roadmap Wrap-Up** (#roadmap-review), Home Depot GDPR compliance (CFR-804) was marked as on track for an **April 15** delivery date. As of the April 2 Slack update from Priya Sharma, it had not yet shipped but remained on schedule. Per the **Q2 2026 OKRs**, closing the Home Depot Enterprise upgrade (post-GDPR delivery) is part of Arjun Mehta's expansion pipeline, representing one of three deals expected to push NRR from 119% to 121%.

Owner: Priya Sharma · Last updated: March 28, 2026.

llmScores.notes: Answer directly addresses the query with specific, well-sourced information about Home Depot pilot status, including delivery date, timeline, and business context.

results.query: What is the latest on the API v1 deprecation timeline?
results.answer:
According to the **API v2 — Launch Plan & v1 Deprecation** document, v1 is set to be sunset on **December 31, 2026**, approximately 12 months after the v2 launch. Migration support will include a dedicated API migration guide and 1:1 sessions for the Top 20 API customers, with **87 customers** currently on v1 (Rajan is tasked with segmenting them by usage volume). Note that this document was last updated **December 20, 2025** — over 90 days ago — so details may have changed, particularly given the API v2 launch was delayed from Q1 to Q3 2026, which could affect the sunset date.

Owner: Rahul V

results.query: What are Apex current pricing tiers?
results.answer:
**Document: Pricing Model Decision — Seat-based vs Usage-based vs Module-based**

Apex uses a **module-based subscription model** (live since April 1, 2025) with three tiers: **Base** (up to 5,000 SKUs, 50K reviews/month — $80K ACV), **Growth** (up to 25,000 SKUs, 250K reviews/month — $160K ACV), and **Enterprise** (unlimited SKUs, unlimited reviews, custom SLA — $250K–$420K ACV). Enterprise customers pay for the Review Engine as a foundation, with optional add-ons including Syndication, Visual UGC, and Insights. Any deal above $250K uses custom enterprise pricing, and 12 existing customers ne
sourceTitles: Pricing Model Decision — Seat-based vs Usage-based vs Module-based

results.query: What revenue did Apex lose before building SSO and which deals were affected?
results.answer: Prior to building SAML 2.0 SSO, Apex lost or pushed **$585K in combined pipeline** across three deals in Q2 2025: Kroger ($220K, blocked by IT security), Estée Lauder ($190K, security review failed and deal pushed to Q3), and Carrefour ($175K, lost to PowerReviews who already had SAML). This analysis was documented by **Ananya Singh** in the **SSO & Enterprise Security — SAML 2.0 Decision** Notion doc dated **August 22, 2025**, and the decision to build SAML (PROD-1623) was approved by CPO Priya Sharma following an escalation from Kavitha Suresh in July 2025.
